title: Controlled irrigation rationing under low-water conditions
summary: The main irrigation water tank is low and replenishment remains uncertain, but current hydraulic conditions still allow a controlled, prioritized irrigation strategy instead of an immediate full stop.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse may not be able to sustain all planned irrigation cycles at normal intensity, but controlled rationing and sector prioritization may preserve continuity until replenishment is secured.
symptoms: The main tank level is low and creates a serious continuity constraint, but the system has not yet reached a confirmed hard-stop condition.
Full planned irrigation demand may not be sustainable during the next cycles unless water use is reduced and prioritized.
Incoming replenishment remains uncertain and cannot be assumed as guaranteed in the short term.
The crop is in production phase, so irrigation should be managed carefully to reduce stress while preserving continuity as much as possible.
A controlled rationing strategy may be operationally preferable to an immediate total interruption if the situation is handled deliberately.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is low enough to threaten normal irrigation continuity, but it does not yet prove that an immediate full stop is unavoidable.
Flow remains stable, which suggests the system can still execute controlled irrigation if demand is reduced and prioritized.
Pressure remains normal, so the issue is scarcity management rather than an immediate hydraulic collapse.
No reliable short-term replenishment commitment is confirmed, which means continuity planning must be cautious and explicit.

operational_context.note: The greenhouse remains in active production and the next irrigation cycles still matter for crop continuity.
operational_context.impact: The service should treat this as a serious operational decision, not as a harmless warning.

operational_context.note: Hydraulic behavior is currently stable enough to allow a temporary controlled response rather than assuming immediate total failure.
operational_context.impact: The service should consider adjustment, prioritization, or escalation with continuity planning before recommending a full stop.

operational_context.note: No guaranteed backup water reserve is confirmed for this situation.
operational_context.impact: The service should avoid optimistic assumptions and remain conservative about duration and continuity risk.

agronomic_context.note: Production-stage tomato crop may tolerate a brief prioritized irrigation strategy better than a full interruption, but stress risk increases if scarcity persists.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should balance short-term continuity management against crop stress and quality deterioration.

business_context.note: A controlled rationing response may protect near-term operational continuity better than an immediate hard stop if managed deliberately and reviewed closely.
business_context.impact: The business risk is not only crop stress, but also choosing an unnecessarily abrupt interruption when a managed continuity response may still be feasible.

notes: This case is designed to test whether Agro-DO can distinguish between a true stop-immediately condition and a serious but still manageable low-water scenario that may justify controlled irrigation rationing, prioritization, or escalation with continuity planning.
